IW Medium Tank

IW Medium Flame Tank

IW Heavy AA Tank
IW Light Tank Destroyer

IW Heavy Tank Destroyer
IW Light SP Artillery
IW Medium SP Artillery
IW Heavy SP Artillery
FT-17 Chassis
T-28 Chassis
T-35 Chassis
FT-17 Derivatives
Small Chassis
The first Russian tank is the MS-1, essentially a copy of  Renault FT-17. The first mass use of FT-17s happened in the last stages of the civil war, as the Russian Army was bolstered by Germans selling their captured French tanks to the Whites. The Lipetsk-Penza Offensive in July 1920 saw Russian-operated FT-17s break through the Red defensive perimeters around Moscow. Based on these experiences, domestic variant of FT-17 was finally created in 1924, under name 'Small Escort Tank-1' - MS-1.
T-26 Series
T-26 Chassis
T-26 is a copy of British Vickers Mark E, designed by Semyon Ginzburg. T-26 and its various versions is the first real success of Russian armour engineering, with T-26 M1934 being essentially modern tank, with a single turret, radio and quick speed. It is primarily intended as infantry and cavalry fire support, though independent T-26 units (some with updated T-46 models) have been formed as part of Separate Mechanised Brigades.
BT Series
BT Chassis
BT line of light tanks is based on American Christie chassis. BTs tend to be fast and pack decent punch, but are woefully lightly armoured. BT became the main tank of the army in early 1930s, and was also used as chassis for various other systems, like the first Russian self-propelled guns, lobbied by Vasily Grabin.
Astrov Series
Astrov Chassis
The Astrov family and the T-50 is spiritual inheritor of the BT series. T-50 and T-60 are decent tanks, though in practice they are outclassed by T-34 in almost everything. They do enjoy support particularly among Cavalry Mechanised Groups of the army for their speed, and attachment to the concept of light tanks even if they are becoming increasingly outgunned in the battlefields.
T-38/40 Series
T-40 Chassis
In Russian armour development, amphibious vehicles hold a special role: in the coming war, armoured vehicles are needed that can cross great Dniepr and Dvina rivers, which form a great part of the German Ostwall. First amphibious experiments were rather mediocre, until through multiple variations T-40 was developed. While it will never be able to go toe-to-toe with 'real' tanks, it is perfected in its singular task of crossing rivers.
Experimental Medium Tanks
Experimental Medium Chassis
Russian medium tanks were designed multi-turreted, which led to constant issues with reliability and overall over-engineering. They went through many variations, with the top-secret project of a heavy armoured light tank being the most wished variant by the military. Shadowing the BT-20 series due to more detailed development, the T-111 still has the foundational problems of early medium tanks, but it can go toe-to-toe with any equivalent German tank.
T-34 Series
T-34 Chassis
T-34 series is the workhorse of Russian armoured forces, being an all-round fast tank developed from the BT-20 project. Reliable single-turret medium tanks are in many ways pinnacle of medium tank development in its simplicity, and Russia managed to reach this stage only through constant trial-and-error. The design is only perfected in the later T-34M tank with its 57-mm gun and lowered profile, which has a lot of improvement for the price of a more complicated production.
T-44 Series
T-44 Chassis
Despite T-34s successes, by later war it became apparent that the tank is being repeatedly outgunned by heavier and more modern enemy variants. Based on frontline experiences, the T-44 series was designed as essentially 'modern tanks' of the Russian Army, with improved armour and gun compared to the T-34M.
T-100 Series
T-100 Chassis
Russian heavy tank development was generally ignored in favour of more mobility-oriented light tank series, and suffered from same multi-turret design problems as the early medium tanks did. T-100 is clear improvement over notoriously unreliable T-35, but nonetheless remains only mediocre heavy tank.
Vityaz Series
Vityaz Chassis
RV (Russian Vityaz) series of heavy tanks - 'The Knights' - are monstrous machines representing the most heavily armoured vehicles in the Russian arsenal. While heavy RV tanks have their situations, they tend to be left in the difficult position of being too unwieldy and heavy to be used in rapid offensives, and outclassed by proper tank destroyers in the defensive role. Still, roaring RVs has a strong psychological effect on the battlefield.
Suvorov Series
AS Chassis
Aleksandr Suvorov series heavy tanks are perhaps the first truly successful Russian heavy tanks, perfected just before the concept is rapidly losing importance against increasing air power and higher performance anti-tank methods. With its heavy armour and powerful weaponry, AS-1 and its successors are designed to break through fortifications like a knife through butter, and in this purpose they excel.
AN
AN Chassis
The only real super-heavy tank to see action in the Russian army is Nikolay Dukhov's design, the Aleksandr Nevsky tank. It is heavy, greatly armoured but lacking in firepower (for its size) and ultimately - like most super-heavy tanks - lacking in real purpose.
T-54 Series
T-54 Chassis
T-54 is further development of T-44, essentially bridging the gap between medium tanks and heavy tanks into a single role. It will continue to serve as the main Russian tank for the rest of 1940s.

Light Tank, Model 1921
Light Tank, Model 1921
M1921
M1921
Without any tanks by the time of France's capitulation in the Weltkrieg, the United States scrambled to develop a stopgap vehicle in the face of the rapidly changing international order. What was ultimately adopted was a Ford-designed near copy of the French FT tank designated the Model of 1921.
Light Tank, Model 2
M2
Alarmed by the rapid evolution of tanks during the Weltkrieg, the United States developed several experimental light tanks during and immediately after the war. While these designs would ultimately lead nowhere, the lessons learned would form the basis for the development of the M2. The M2 possesses many features copied directly from Canadian tanks and is armed with a single high-velocity 37mm cannon.
Light Tank, Model 3
M3
The shortcomings of the M2 were quickly realised by American forces and work was soon begun on a new completely redesigned variant. While the project was interrupted by the American Civil War, engineers loyal to the Federal Government would eventually complete the project.
Light Tank, Model 24
M24
While inspired by the M3, the M24 is an entirely new design developed with special consideration for performance in rough terrain.

Medium Tank, Model 3
M3
While American efforts to develop a modern medium tank on the basis of an enlarged variant of the M2 fractured with the onset of the civil war, the Federal government, motivated by the war, brought such plans quickly to fruition thereafter. The resulting M3 tank was the first entirely domestically designed medium tank in service with any faction of the American Civil War.

Heavy Tank, Model 28
T28
The T28 Super Heavy Tank was designed around an experimental 105mm gun equipped with a cutting edge autoloader system. With armour up to slightly over twelve inches thick in the front, the T8 was quite slow, but able to with reliably resist even relatively powerful anti-tank cannons.
Medium Tank, Model 46
M46
A slightly smaller derivative of the M26 tank, the M46 addressed many of that vehicles reliability issues. Agile, heavily armoured, and equipped with an ultra-high velocity 105mm gun, the M46 was the king of the battlefield.

$USA_medium_tank_equipment_0$
$USA_medium_tank_equipment_0_short$
Medium Tank, Model 3
M3 Grant
While American efforts to develop a modern medium tank on the basis of an enlarged variant of the M2 fractured with the onset of the civil war, the Provisional Government of New England, motivated by the war, brought such plans quickly to fruition thereafter. Taking inspiration from the Canadian medium tank, the A15 Ram - the M3 features a Canadian developed pattern turrent. The M3 Grant also features a bussel rack at the back of its turrent to accommodate for a mobile radio transceiver.
Medium Tank, Model 4
M4 Sherman
Inspired by the tank doctrine of the Canadians as well as personal experiences, the American military sacrificed the M3's side sponson mount for a 75 mm gun in a fully traversing central turret. The designers behind the M4 stressed the tank's mechanical relaibility, ease of production, maintenance, durability, standardisation, and moderation of weight and size. The M4's relative ease of production combined with the industrial might of America will allow the M4 to be rapidly produced with repair units bringing disabled vehicles to be repaired quickly.
Medium Tank, Model 20
M20 Clark
Named after New Englander general Mark Clark, the M20 represents the latest devleopment in American armoured warfare. The M20 is an improvement over the M4, featuring heavier hull armour (0.5 inches/13mm thicker)

$USA_amphibious_tank_equipment_2_short$
$USA_self_propelled_super_heavy_artillery_equipment_1$
$USA_self_propelled_super_heavy_artillery_equipment_1_short$
Rock Island Arsenal, Light Tank, Model 1921
Rock Island Arsenal, Light Tank, Model 1921
RIA-L1921
RIA-L1921
Without any tanks by the time of France's capitulation in the Weltkrieg, the United States scrambled to develop a stopgap vehicle in the face of the rapidly changing international order. What was ultimately adopted was a Ford-designed near copy of the French FT tank designated the Model of 1921.
Rock Island Arsenal, Light Tank, Model 2
RIA-L2
Alarmed by the rapid evolution of tanks during the Weltkrieg, the United States developed several experimental light tanks during and immediately after the war. While these designs would ultimately lead nowhere, the lessons learned would form the basis for the development of the M2. The M2 possesses many features copied directly from Canadian tanks and is armed with a single high-velocity 37mm cannon. Despite being the first light tank in $WCA_ACW$ service, the vehicle was designated L2 (L for light) upon the creation of the new nation's army.
Rock Island Arsenal, Light Tank, Model 3
RIA-L3
While the Federal Government and People's Government developed improved variants of the M2 light tank along similar lines, the $WCA_ACW$ insted endeavoured to maintain the M2's profile while improving every element individually. The resulting L3 was the first vehicle designed and produced entirely by the socialist regime in Chicago.
Continental Army, Light Tank, Model 5
CA-L5
Essentially a scaled-down version of the ill-fated M8 tank, the L5 was the first socialist-produced light tank not based upon the pre-war M2. Armed with a new high-velocity French-designed 75mm gun, the L5 garnered a powerful reputation among its operators and foes alike.

Medium Tank, Model 1

Continental Army, Medium Tank, Model 4
CA-M4
The initial socialist efforts to develop a medium tank fell apart amidst fierce inter-union rivalries. With help from Socialist nations abroad however, the $WCA_ACW$ eventually managed to develop what is in essence a scaled-up L2 tank. While not an exceptional vehicle, the M4 would serve admirably in a variety of roles.
Continental Army, Medium Tank, Model 5
CA-M5
Heavily inspired by both French designs and the People's Government's 'Hood' tank, the M5 would serve as the backbone of Syndicates armoured divisions for much of the American Civil War. Fast and possessing thick frontal armour, the M5 proved a tough nut for its adversaries to crack.
Continental Army, Medium Tank, Model 9
CA-M9
After the plant responsible for development of the WCA's proposed M8 medium tank fell to Federal forces, the design was taken overly directly by the Central Design Bureau of the $WCA_ACW$ armed forces and refined. Like its Federal counterpart, the M9 mounts a 90mm main cannon.

Medium Tank Prototype, Model 2

Continental Army, Heavy Tank, Model 1
CA-H1
The H1 heavy tank began its development not as a tank, but as a commercial tractor. With the American Civil War's outbreak, the new union responsible for the as of yet unreleased tractor's development was ordered to retool for military production. On the basis of this almost forgotten tractor, the H1 was eventually developed. Though plagued with engine problems and armour only just sufficient for its role, the H1 was the first heavy to be deployed in large numbers during the war and had a substantial impact.
Continental Army, Heavy Tank, Model 2
CA-H2
Though externally visually similar to the H1, the H2 project actually predates the H1. A product of the Central Military Design Bureau of the $WCA_ACW$, with the successful deployment of the H1, the precursor to the H2 was redesigned for familiarity for H1 crews. Though at times slow and hard to manoeuvre, the H2's main cannon packs a formidable punch and its armour without compare at the time of its initial deployment.
Continental Army, Heavy Tank, Model 4
CA-H4
The H4 was borne from a need for a heavy tank agile enough to work more effectively with lighter vehicles. While the H4's armour was not much thicker than that on the H2, the vehicle was indeed substantially faster, especially in harsh conditions, and armed with a gun capable of taking on enemy heavy tanks with ease.

Continental Army, Heavy Tank, Model 5
CA-H5
While the H2 heavy tank excelled in its role, its armament and armour were found to be insufficient, especially for urban environments. To accommodate a larger engine, new 105mm gun, and thicker plating, the chassis was lengthened and a new turret designed. The up-armouring done to the vehicle was substantial, and while capable of withstanding direct hits from large-calibre guns, the H5 was quite sluggish.
Continental Army, Medium Tank, Model 12
CA-M12
Originally a development upon the M9 medium tank, due to the radical changes made, the M12 was eventually reclassified as an entirely new type of tank capable of filling the role of a both a medium and heavy tank. The pride of the regime, the M12 leaves only the destroyed remnants of its enemies in its wake.

$USA_self_propelled_super_heavy_artillery_equipment_1$
$USA_self_propelled_super_heavy_artillery_equipment_1_short$
Light Tank, Model 1921
Light Tank, Model 1921
M1921 Light
M1921 Light
Without any tanks by the time of France's capitulation in the Weltkrieg, the United States scrambled to develop a stopgap vehicle in the face of the rapidly changing international order. What was ultimately adopted was a Ford-designed near copy of the French FT tank designated the Model of 1921.
Light Tank, Model 1935
M1935 Light
Alarmed by the rapid evolution of tanks during the Weltkrieg, the United States developed several experimental light tanks during and immediately after the war. While these designs would ultimately lead nowhere, the lessons learned would form the basis for the development of the M1935. The M1935 possesses many features copied directly from Canadian tanks and is armed with a single high-velocity 37mm cannon.
Light Tank, Model 1939
M1939 Light
The shortcomings of the M1935 were quickly realised by American forces and work was soon begun on a new completely redesigned variant. While the project was interrupted by the American Civil War, engineers in $APG_ACW$ would eventually complete the project. The unofficial designation 'Stuart', after Confederate General J.E.B Stuart, was retained by the People's Government.
Light Tank, Model 1941
M1941 Light
Designed around the revised three inch gun introduced with the M1940 tank, the M1941 was built from the ground up for speed and agility. While problems with the tank's cutting-edge engine delayed the project, the M1941 would eventually become the standard light tank of the People's Government.

Medium Tank, Model 1
Medium M1
Medium Tank, Model 1939
M1939 Medium
American efforts to develop a modern medium tank on the basis of an enlarged variant of the M2 tank were fractured with the civil war. While the People's Government struggled to develop the, 1940 tank, it reverse engineered captured Federal M3 tanks as a stopgap.
Medium Tank, Model 1940
M1940 Medium
The M1940 was a monumentous effort for the People's Government's fledgling armed forces. While not particularly agile or heavily armoured, the M4 excelled in a variety of roles and its new three inch main gun proved exceptional both against infantry and armour.
Medium Tank, Model 1940A6
M1940A6 Medium
The product of a series of improvements upon the M1940 tank, the M1940A6 came into being with the adaptation of a new 105mm anti-tank gun to the M1940 platform. While the gun proved more than capable of defeating even the most heavily armoured vehicles during the American Civil War, it likewise was difficult to operate for M1940A6 crews, dampening its effectiveness.

Heavy Tank, Model 1937
M1937 Heavy
The process development of the M1937 heavy tank was marred by setbacks as the People's Government struggled to design a heavy tank from the ground up. The vehicle that was eventually produce, however, proved capable and the lessons learned were critical to the success of later projects for the then fledgling APG.
Heavy Tank, Model 1941
M1941 Heavy
Conceived as an entirely new type of vehicle specialised for assaulting fortified positions, the vehicle was the first and only 'Assault Tank' adopted by the People's Government. Essentially a slightly enlarged medium tank with substantially improved armour, especially on the sides and rear, the M1941 proved insufficient in roles other than that for which it was designed.

Heavy Tank, Model 1943
M1943 Heavy
Heavy fixed fortifications proved difficult for other People's Government armoured vehicles to overcome. The M105-2 was built specifically for the destruction of fortifications and armour capable of withstanding direct hits from large stationary guns. While slow, the vehicle's 105mm cannon proved adequete against structures and soft targets alike.
Medium Tank, Model 1946
M1946 Medium
Designed as a small heavy tank able to fit in automobile and railway tunnels, the M1946 gradually came to fill a variety of roles. Fast and equipped with a massive 120mm gun, the M1946 lacks only in protection compared to heavier vehicles of the same era.

CLR T15
T15
While Germany was at first miffed by the Flandro-Walloon selection of a highly modified Canadian Vicker's Mark III for its first light tank since the Weltkrieg-era FT, fears of creeping Canadian influence were squashed when the country placed substantial orders for German armaments, engines, and more for the new vehicle. After some initial frustrations, the vehicle would enter service as the T15.
$GER_light_tank_equipment_2$
$GER_light_tank_equipment_2_short$
$GER_light_tank_equipment_3$
$GER_light_tank_equipment_3_short$
SA F.R.C. 76mm
SA 76mm
The SA 76 is a peculiar looking vehicle if ever there was one. Little more than a Canadian Vickers tankette fitted with a comically oversized 76mm gun, this ugly duckling packs a mighty punch despite glaring weaknesses.
$GER_light_tank_artillery_equipment_2$
$GER_light_tank_artillery_equipment_2_short$
$GER_light_tank_artillery_equipment_3$
$GER_light_tank_artillery_equipment_3_short$
T13
T13
Due to the favourable reception of Canadian Vickers tankettes and German pressure for Flandro-Walloon government to sever ties with Canadian manufacturers, when the need arose to adopt a tank destroyer, a design from a small arms manufacturer near Hainaut was selected due mostly to its similarities to familiar Canadian designs. The vehicle is armed with the same 47mm anti-tank gun already in service with the army.
T13 B3
T13 B3
The T13 B3, while indeed an evolution of older T13 designs, bears little resemblance to those vehicles. Sporting both a turret and new 75mm gun on a completely redesigned chassis, the B3 may as well be a new vehicle entirely.

AMO-Sisu BT-10
BT-10
In 1935, following an error in production and given Russian financial problems, a small number of defective Russian BT-10 tanks were sold to Finland, where they were retrofitted and completed. The Finnish BT-10 is armed with the same 37mm Bofors gun as the Mk E, adopted mere months prior.

DAF M39 Pantserwagen
DAF M39
For its time the DAF M39 was a modern design with an all-welded monocoque construction of the hull and extensive use of sloped armour. The turret, fitted with a relatively powerful 37 mm cannon, was produced in Sweden by Landsverk. The type was lightly armoured and relatively fast, with a good cross-country capability.

Zırhlı V
Zırhlı V
Z-V
Z-V
During the Weltkrieg, Ottoman army captured Mark V and FT tanks then refitted them into service, dubbing them 'Zırhlı V' and 'MT-17'. After British forces surrendered, Ottoman Empire received a large number of Mark V as spoils of war. The Z-V tank was a main tank in the Ottoman armoured corps. Though more advanced tanks replace the Z-Vs eventually, army still consider these for training its tank crews.
Muharebe Tankı Kalıp 1352
MT-52 'Sekban'
MT-52 Sekban project is the first attempt to reorganise and reform the Ottoman Armoured Corps that was included in Harb-i Yıldırım military reforms. To this end, the Ottoman Empire bought a licence for the German Spähpanzer II and designed a production line that Ottoman factories could handle. The initial plan was to have the MT-52 to replace old Z-Vs, however field tests showed that the MT-52 did not match the level of German production. Nevertheless, the quality of the new tank was accepted as good enough.
Muharebe Tankı Kalıp 1355
MT-55 'Beylik'
MT-55 Beylik is the second significant effort to modernise the Ottoman Armoured Corps. Errors and reliability problems of MT-52 were mostly fixed while additional modifications were made to the cannon, hull and engine with help of the German advisors.
I. Hafif Zırhlı Muharebe Vasıtası
I.HZMV 'Tatar'
I. Hafif Zırhlı Muharebe Vasıtası 'Tatar' is a lighter variant of the II. ZMV Uygur developed in the same time. It was designed for scouting and flanking purposes on base of the German Sturmpanzerkampfwagen IV and other German tanks, using the experience of the MT-55 development.

$GER_medium_tank_equipment_0$
$GER_medium_tank_equipment_0_short$
I. Zırhlı Muharebe Vasıtası
I.ZMV 'Uygur'
The I. Zırhlı Muharebe Vasıtası (First Armoured Battle Vehicle) 'Uygur' is a tank developed on the basis of the German Sturmpanzerkampfwagen IV along with the MT line. It have a thicker hull and also have improved cannon and armour design compared with the MT-55. I. ZMV have many variants thanks to the fact that its chassis is easily upgradeable, as demanded by Commissioner Ömer Fahrettin Türkkan who wanted its design and parts to be simple and easily interchangeable.
II. Zırhlı Muharebe Vasıtası
II.ZMV 'Kuman'
The II. ZMV 'Kuman' medium tank is an updated version of I. ZMV. It's designed to withstand the harshest conditions while retaining a decent combat efficiency. It have 7.7 cm ÇVT main armament and M1358 coaxial MGs. Experimental sloped armour and armoured skirts were also introduced in an attempt to counter incoming enemy AT rounds.
III. Zırhlı Muharebe Vasıtası
III.ZMV 'Altay'
Altay is the common name of a medium tank developed by Ottoman Empire during Second Weltkrieg and late stages of Second Arab Revolt. It's intended to standardise tank production, and as a replacement for the I. ZMV and II. ZMV. While have a too complex design to replace them on battlefields, the Altay tank quickly found a niche in combat tactics of the Ottoman Military. The Germans dubbed the Altay Tank 'Clausewitz Pasha' because its design was heavily influenced by the German Panzerkampfwagen V von Clausewitz.
$GER_medium_tank_artillery_equipment_0$
$GER_medium_tank_artillery_equipment_0_short$
I. Kendinden Tahrikli Top
I.KTT 'Cihadiye'
I. Kendinden Tahrikli Top 'Cihadiye' is a self propelled medium artillery 125mm cannon mounted on a I. ZMV's chassis, I. ZKMT SPG packs respectable firepower while still being quite mobile.
II. Kendinden Tahrikli Top
II.KTT 'Mehmediye'
Mehmediye is a SPG with improved 128mm cannon mounted on II. ZMV's chassis, production started during early stages of Arab Revolt.
III. Kendinden Tahrikli Top
III.KTT 'Hamidiye'
Hamidiye is a prototype medium SPG on a Altay tank chassis, this advanced medium SPG that had good range and firepower. However due to complexity of its design, the Altay project needed to be abandoned along with the other variant types of it.

$GER_medium_tank_aa_equipment_0$
$GER_medium_tank_aa_equipment_0_short$
I. Motorlu Tayyare Topu
I.MTT Yörük
I. Motorlu Tayyare Topu Yörük is medium SPAA developed during Harb-i Yıldırım military reforms. It was intended for escorting armoured assault battalions against incoming enemy aircraft, while also providing fire support to friendly forces if necessary.
II. Motorlu Tayyare Topu
II.MTT 'Çerkez'
Çerkez is a mobile anti-air artillery mounted on a II. ZMV chassis. Its main armament consists of four 30mm Kuşçu AA guns on open turret and M35 as coaxial MG.
III. Motorlu Tayyare Topu
III.MTT 'Türkmen'
Prototype SP anti air artillery that was designed during Altay project. However project itself had to be cancelled due to its costly and complex design.

Char d'Assaut Renault FT Modèle 1917 Modifié 29
Char d'Assaut Renault FT Modèle 1917 Modifié 29
FT-29
FT-29
The FT was the first production tank to have its armament within a fully rotating turret and was revolutionary in terms of tank designs, later copied by other countries. Renault's design, developed in 1917, was technically far more advanced than the other two French tanks of the Weltkrieg. The French army, after retreat to Algeria, modified remaining tanks with the Hotchkiss Mle 26 machine gun in 1929, but in general, many of the FTs were sold to other countries to support the French budget.
Char de Manœuvre Renault Modèle 1930
CMR-30
In 1926, Renault has obtained an order to build two new prototypes as a parallel modification project, with the factory designation Renault NC. In 1930, Renault succeeded in his attempt to get his Renault NC accepted as the basis for further light infantry tank development, the final result of which was adopted by the army in 1932, under the name Char de Manœuvre Renault Modèle 1930. A new tank based largely on revision of the original design of the Renault FT, and armed with a Puteaux SA Mle 18 cannon and two Hotchkiss Mle 26 machine guns. By request of the French army, the tanks have a radio operator's assigned position, and are equipped with a huge and characteristic twin-boom antenna. Due to this unusual feature, the tower has a reduced traverse of approximately 345°.
Char de Manœuvre d'Ensemble Renault Modèle 1935
CMER-35
With the development of the CMR-30 tank, which had already departed from purely infantry support, the French army ordered a new tank that could fight enemy counterparts. Renault requested funding for a development of the new tank that was turned down, until they presented a prototype. The Renault UZ prototype was introduced in 1935 and immediately was accepted in service as Char de Manœuvre d'Ensemble Renault Modèle 1935 due to financial problems of the French government, but in limited numbers. In many ways, the CMER-35 is a slightly enlarged model of the CMR-30, retaining many of the features of the previous vehicle, but with increased armour and armed with a 47mm cannon.
Char Léger de Combat Citroën
CLC-C
The Char Léger de Combat Citroën is a light tank created by the request of the French Army for a lightly armoured heavy combat vehicle. The CLC-C has an oscillating turret with a cupola for the gunner, located along the turret centreline. The turret itself is offset to the left, in order to make room for a driver's compartment in the hull, and is armed with a magazine 90-mm cannon on the right side. Of the significant drawbacks of the new tank, it can be noted that the breech is located outside the turret, which means that the gunner can reload it only by venturing outside of the tank.

Char de Bataille Peugeot Modèle 1933
CBP-33
Char de Bataille Peugeot Modèle 1939
CBP-39
The Char de Bataille Peugeot Modèle 1939 is an upgraded compound of the CMER-35 and the CRP-37S. Developed by Peugeot in preparation for the return to France, the CBP-39 is armed with a 75mm gun and good armour. However, the CBP-39 does not have a special niche, and it is mainly used to fill holes in the tactical and strategic plans of the French command.
Char de Combat Général-1 Renault
CdC G1-R
The CdC G1-R is a 20-ton replacing for the CMER-35. The CdC G1-R is one of the most advanced French tanks, providing good armament and mobility and suitable for multiple roles. Armed with 75mm gun and featuring improved protection, the tank initially suffered from low reliability, but after several changes in the specifications, the CdC G1-R was adopted in service. Considering as multi-role tank, the CdC G1-R have several innovative parts such as a gun stabilisation and semi-automatic loading systems and an optical rangefinder.
Char de Combat Général-2 Peugeot
CdC G2-P
The CdC G2-P is a next-generation tank, designed to replace the CdC G1-R. The powerful engine and low weight provide the new vehicle with excellent mobility, and the 90-mm gun with mechanised ammunition rack gives a good rate of fire. However, significant size with rather weak armour, as well as a very complex and expensive design, are the main disadvantages of the new tank.

FCM 2C
2C
Char de Rupture Peugeot Modèle 1937 tourelle S
CRP-37S
Designed to break through the Communard defences, the Char de Rupture Peugeot Modèle 1937 is equipped with two main armaments: a 75mm gun in body and a 47mm one in tower. The turret changed a lot during development and the final version, known as the 'tourelle S', is close to the CMR-30 turret. Despite the cost and weight of the new tank, the CRP-37S was accepted into service, and is seen as necessary in the plans of the French command.
Char d'Attaque des Fortifications Citroën Modèle 1941
CAFC-41
Designed to the same specifications as the CRP-37S, the CAFC-41 was rejected due to its high cost and weight, even compared to the CRP-37S. Equipped with the same armament as the CRP-37S, the new tank entered service only in 1941 after several modifications. The 1941 model is almost identical to the 1937 one, but the transmission and exhaust moved centrally and the 47mm gun in the tower is replaced with a 75mm gun.
Platforme Blindée d'Attaque des Fortifications Standardisé
PBAFS
The PBAFS, officially called the Platforme Blindée d'Attaque des Fortifications Standardisé-Citroën, originally was designed as a competition to the CdC G1-R, but was rejected for being too heavy. Adopted 2 years later after significant modifications, such as replacing the 75mm tower gun with a 90mm one and increasing the armour, the goal of the PBAFS is to break through enemy fortifications and support lighter tanks from a distance, due to still having too weak armour for direct combat with enemy heavy tanks.

Char Forteresse Peugeot Modèle 1943
CFP-43
The Char Forteresse Peugeot Modèle 1943 is an extremely heavy and long tank with 120mm armour, 8 crew members, and the ability to cross trenches up to 7 metres wide. The development of the CFP-43, equipped with a 105mm main cannon and a 47mm secondary cannon, was extremely challenging, with constantly changing requirements. Initially adopted in 1941, the final version was introduced in 1943, and entered service as it was necessary to break through even larger enemy fortifications than other tanks can.
Chasseur de Char CFP-43
CDC CFP-43

Amphibie CFP-43
Amphibie CFP-43
Char de Combat Général-3
CdC G3
As part of the discussion on the standardisation of French tanks and the replacement of the CdC G2-P and CdC G1-R tanks, the Char de Combat Général-3 design was proposed. Conceived as an assault tank that can be transferred by rail, the CdC G3 suffers from many faults such as weak armour and low reliability, despite good 105mm main gun and having a speed of 50 km/h.
Chasseur de Char G3
CDC G3
G3 155mm

Amphibie CDC G3
Amphibie CDC G3
Char Léger Amphibie Schneider-Citroën
CLA-SC
Developed on the C-40 chassis, the CLA-SC is a rather weak amphibious tank with a mixed track-and-wheeled chassis and a CMR-30 turret. Introduced into service due to the need for amphibious tanks in preparation for the return of France, the CLA-SC has weak armour and low speed, but is quite reliable.
Char de Manœuvre Amphibie Modèle 1942
CdMA-42
The CdMA-42, developed by request of the French army, is primarily an amphibious 'tank hunter' armed with a 90mm cannon. Hydraulic skis placed under the hull provide good manoeuvrability in marshes and snow terrain, while balloons located around give the vessel the ability to float. Despite several faults, the CdMA-42 is a capable amphibious tank with good speed and firepower, and replaced the CLA-SC in service.

Vickers A1, Light
A1
Vickers A1, Light
A1
Vickers A4 'Reliant'
A4 'Reliant'
The Vickers Mark E entered limited service with the Canadian Army in 1932 with the designation 'A4' as a stopgap tankette while more advanced light tanks were developed. It was given the rather ironic nickname 'Reliant' for its simultaneous reliability in harsh terrain and tendency to breakdown randomly in seemingly innocuous situations.

$IMP_amphibious_tank_equipment_2$
$IMP_amphibious_tank_equipment_2_short$
Burstyn Motorgeschütze
Burstyn MtG
Burstyn Motorgeschütze
Burstyn MtG
Škoda Leichter Kampfwagen Modell 1935
leKpfW M.35
In 1933, the k.u.k. Army realised that its incoherent tank fleet consisting of older foreign tank designs and domestic tankettes is rapidly losing combat effectiveness against new syndicalist and German designs. As a stop-gap measure, it decided to adopt the new Škoda Š-II export tank. The modern design was equipped with a two-man turret with 37mm gun and pneumatic gearbox and running gears, which was the tanks biggest problem due to its complexity and low reliability.
ČKD Leichter Kampfwagen Modell 38
leKpfW M.38
Even as the first leKpfW M.35 were coming off the production line, the army was already looking for new cavalry tank. In the following design competition multiple domestic and foreign designs were considered, but in the end, the army chose one of the TNH models from ČKD. Just as with the M.35, TNHP had a two-man turret and an improved 37mm gun, but the M.38 improved on the M.35's armour, engine and possessed much simpler and reliable running gears. Unfortunately due to Black-Monday, the first units wouldn't enter service until 1938.
ČKD Leichter Kampfwagen Modell 42
leKpfW M.42
While the heavier M.40 replaced M.38 in the main combat role, there was still a need for a smaller and lighter tracked reconnaissance vehicle. To keep track with the quick pace of war development, ČKD completely redesigned the upper part of the TNHP design. The new reconnaissance tank had improved tracks, sloped frontal armour and new turret housing the commander, gunner and the brand new autoloading 57mm gun developed by Škoda.
Škoda Leichter Panzerjägerwagen Modell 36
lePzJgW M.36
ČKD Leichter Panzerjägerwagen Modell 38
lePzJgW M.38
ČKD Leichter Panzerjägerwagen Modell 42
lePzJgW M.42

leGW M.36
ČKD Leichter Geschützwagen Modell 38
leGW M.38
ČKD Leichter Geschützwagen Modell 42
leGW M.42
Škoda Leichter Flugabwehrkanonewagen Modell 36
leFlakW M.36

leFlakW M.38
ČKD Leichter Flugabwehrkanonewagen Modell 42
leFlakW M.42
Flammpanzer Modell 36
Flamm.Pz. M.36
Flammpanzer Modell 38
Flamm.Pz. M.38
Flammpanzer Modell 42
Flamm.Pz. M.42
Schwimmpanzer Modell 36
Schwimm.Pz. M.36
Schwimmpanzer Modell 38
Schwimm.Pz. M.38
Schwimmpanzer Modell 42
Schwimm.Pz. M.42
Škoda-MÁVAG Mittlerer Kampfwagen Modell 34
mKpfW M.34
Škoda-MÁVAG Mittlerer Kampfwagen Modell 40
mKpfW M.40
Experience from Second American civil war, China and Spain would lead the K.u.K. army command to order a new tank with larger 3 man turret, a 75mm gun and further resilient frontal armour. The final project design was a joint venture of the Czech Škoda providing hull and the Hungarian MAVÁG building the turret and gun.
Škoda-MÁVAG Mittlerer Kampfwagen Modell 40/42
mKpfW M.42
While at the start of the war the armament and armour of M.40 were more than sufficient, the introduction of infantry held AT weapons using shaped charges and the increasing armour thickness of enemy tanks marked the M.40 as nearly obsolete by the end of 1941. In response, Škoda's engineers drew up a new design with a similar base but possessing an entirely new engine, armoured side skirts and stronger frontal armour. MAVÁG would provide a completely new turret while also improving crew space and the field of vision within the vehicle altogether with long barrel 75 mm gun.
Weiss Manfréd Mittlerer Kampfwagen Modell 44
mKpfW M.44
Heavily influenced by German Kriegsengel and Russian Obukhov tanks, the M.44 was the Military Technical Committee's response to the army's need for replacement of the quickly ageing M.40/42 tanks. The design included sloped frontal and side armour, ČKD's improved suspension prototype, Austro-Daimler's V12 engine and a licence-built 80mm Bofors gun. The Hungarian Weiss Manfréd firm would build the hulls and assemble the machines.
Weiss Manfréd Mittlerer Panzerjägerwagen Modell 34

Škoda Infanteriepanzer Modell 32
IPz M.32
Škoda Infanteriepanzer Modell 36
IPz M.36
With the introduction of the leKpfW M.35, the army also decided to acquire an infantry tank variant to aid breakthroughs. Multiple models were considered, each with its own pros and cons. In the end, the military settled on combining the multi-crew turret from the Škoda Š-III with the reliable hull of the Tatra T-III.
Austro-Daimler-Porsche Infanteriepanzer Modell 42
IPz M.42
As western front settled into a stalemate just a few weeks after the start of the war, the K.u.K. army posted specifications and opened offers for a new infantry tank design. With the various Czech and Hungarian companies being busy with cavalry tanks, Ferdinand von Porsche's design was the only one submitted in time and would be produced by Austro-Daimler. Equipped with 100mm frontal armour and an 80mm gun from Bofors this tank was feared by any who had to fight against it. Due to the design's use of unreliable electric-powered wheels, it was also much feared by its own crew.
Austro-Daimler-Porsche Infanteriepanzer Modell 44
IPz M.44
Model M.44 was an overall improvement of M.42. Its frontal armour was now sloped, the cramped turret was redesigned into a new much larger one, which was also able to fit the new 105mm Škoda gun. In addition, the problems with the 42's electric running gears would be tackled and solved. Mostly.

Austro-Daimler-Porsche Landschlachtschiff Franzl
Franzl
With the war raging across Europe, the Military Technical Committee worried about the necessity of a breakthrough tank capable of taking on heavy fortifications in Italy or France. A monstrous design by Austro-Daimler's Ferdinand von Porsche and Skoda engineers was proposed, ironically gaining the diminutive nickname of \

ČKD-Škoda Standardpanzer Modell 50
StPz M.50
The TVP hull was first designed by ČKD as a competitor to Weiss Manfréd's M.44 tank during the design competition for the contract to replace the ageing fleet of M.40/42s, where it would be rejected due to its larger dimensions. However, as the war progressed, army high command would issue a directive mandating the adoption of Škoda's new autoloading 105mm gun by lighter tanks in response to the heavily armoured series Russian T-120 and French M4 tanks and, as a result of its previously unfortunate size, only the TVP hull was sufficiently large enough for the role.
Jagdpanzer Modell 50
JgPz M.50
Geschützpanzer Modell 50
GPz M.50
Flugabwehrkanonepanzer Modell 50
FlakPz M.50

Schwimmpanzer Modell 50
Schwimm.Pz. M.50
ČKD F-IV-H
F-IV-H
In the 1930s, the Military Technical Committee became interested in an amphibious tank capable of operating across the Monarchy's many rivers and around the Adriatic. ČKD would provide a variant of its AH-IV tankette, the F-IV-H, for the role. It only had a machine gun for armament, but the design would only be surpassed in 1940 by Miklos Straussler's revolutionary \
Leták
Leták
Analysis of airborne operations during the war clearly demonstrated the need for armoured support for paratroopers. Early attempts addressed the size and weight constraints necessary to fit cargo planes, only to be delayed by new demands for amphibious capabilities. The resulting design was a sleek, low-profile light tank with a capable 57mm autocannon.
